title: L02.4.naming rules
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making Lecture 2.4: Naming rules and conventions

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

Rules and conventions

Rules and conventions are important to follow when writing code:

Rules: Define what you are allowed to do Conventions: Define what you should do and, more importantly, what others expect you to have done.

The Python community treats conventions seriously.

“PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” is an essential reference for Python coding conventions.

A consistent, disciplined approach that follows established rules and conventions will help you and others understand and maintain your code e!ectively.

Python: Variable names  Rules: Naming

Naming rules apply to any variable or function we create.

Names must start with a letter or underscore (“_“). Names can only contain alphanumeric characters and underscores. Names are case-sensitive.

As long as you follow these rules, you can use nearly anything as a name, however. . .

Python: Variable names

The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

Variable names are written in lower-case Words are separated with underscores

It is best to be expressive when naming variables, clearly describing the information to which the variable relates.

For example, when naming a variable reflecting the price of a call option: Best: call_option_price Bad: calloptionprice Worse: cop

Python: Variable names - Rule (Reserved keywords)

Python reserved keywords cannot be used for variable or function names. These include:

False await else import pass None break except in raise True class finally is return and continue for lambda try as def from nonlocal while assert del global not with async elif if or yield

Using a reserved keyword as a variable name results in a syntax error.

Python: Variable names - Convention (Built-in function names)

Python built-in function names should not be used for variable or function names. These include: 1 abs enumerate list slice 2 aiter eval locals sorted 3 all exec map staticmethod 4 anext filter max str 5 any float memoryview sum 6 ascii format min super 7 bin frozenset next tuple 8 bool getattr object type 9 breakpoint globals oct vars 10 bytearray hasattr open zip 11 bytes hash ord 12 callable help pow 13 chr hex print 14 classmethod id property 15 compile input range 16 complex int repr 17 delattr isinstance reversed 18 dict issubclass round 19 dir iter set 20 divmod len setattr

Using a built-in function name as a variable name can be confusing and may lead to unexpected errors.

Python: Variable names - Convention (Single leading underscores)

Python programmers typically use a single leading underscore to indicate that a variable or function should be treated as private.

A leading underscore name starts with an underscore: _var or _method. Private variables and methods are “internal” to a module. They are not intended for users of a module. Using a leading underscore to indicate something is private is only a convention; it is not part of the language’s syntax.

You should only use a leading underscore when you intend to treat the variable or method as private.

Python: Variable names - Rule (Double leading underscores)

The Python interpreter treats variables beginning with a double underscore specially.

Names with leading double underscores, like __var or __method, will be mangled in some cases. This changes their names internally so they are hard to access within the program. Other names like __file__ or __init__ have special meaning and follow a “magic name” convention.

Avoid using double underscores unless you clearly understand how they work.

Python: Variable names - Best practices

Combining “PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” with the previous rules suggests

Variables and methods should be written in “snake case” – lower-case letters with words separated by underscores: e.g., my_standard_variable (PEP 8) Constant variables (variables that do not change as a program executes) should be written in “screaming snake case” – capitalised letters throughout with words separated by underscores: e.g., MY_CONSTANT_VARIABLE (PEP 8) Do not use reserved keywords or built-in function names for variables. Do not start a variable name with a single underscore unless it is intended to be private. Never start a variable name with two underscores.

Key takeaways

Name regular variables in snake_case Name constant variables in SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE Do not use reserved keywords of built-in function names Limit the use of single leading underscores and double leading underscores
